threats.title: Incorrect Epoch Boundary Calculation in Reward Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: The update_market function incorrectly calculates nextEpoch as `i + BLOCK_EPOCH` instead of `epoch + BLOCK_EPOCH`. This causes the loop to skip actual epoch boundaries and apply the wrong cantoPerBlock rate to blocks spanning multiple epochs.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental logic error in the core reward distribution math. It systematically misallocates rewards, allowing attackers to strategically time updates to extend high-reward rates into low-reward epochs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: nextEpoch calculated as i + BLOCK_EPOCH instead of epoch + BLOCK_EPOCH

preconditions: update_market is called when lastRewardBlock is not perfectly aligned with an epoch boundary

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: nextEpoch overshoots the actual epoch boundary, causing blockDelta to include blocks from the next epoch while using the current epoch's reward rate
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that nextEpoch aligns with an actual epoch boundary

analysis_questions: Does this calculation error allow users to steal rewards by timing their calls?
How much value can be extracted if epoch rates change significantly?
validation_requirements: Trace the loop execution with a mid-epoch lastRewardBlock
Calculate the exact reward difference between the buggy implementation and the correct epoch-aligned implementation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker waits for a high-reward epoch to end. They delay calling update_market until mid-way through the next (low-reward) epoch. The bug applies the high-reward rate to the blocks in the low-reward epoch, granting the attacker excess rewards.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol insolvency due to over-emitting rewards beyond configured rates
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users and the protocol treasury
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade; excess emitted rewards cannot be recovered

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for update_market spanning multiple epochs with varying reward rates and mid-epoch lastRewardBlock

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily visible on-chain via emitted reward amounts

threats.title: Silent Downcast Truncation of accCantoPerShare Causes Reward Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: The update_market function explicitly casts the accumulator increment to uint128. In Solidity 0.8.x, explicit downcasting silently truncates values exceeding the target type's maximum. A small marketSupply combined with normal cantoReward can exceed type(uint128).max, wrapping the accumulator.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the monotonicity of the reward accumulator. If it wraps, existing users permanently lose their unclaimed rewards because their rewardDebt will be higher than the new wrapped accumulatedCanto.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Explicit downcast uint128() on a potentially large uint256 value

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: market.accCantoPerShare += uint128(...) silently truncates instead of reverting
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No SafeCast library used for downcasting

analysis_questions: Can an attacker intentionally manipulate marketSupply to trigger this?
What happens to users' rewardDebt when the accumulator wraps?
validation_requirements: Verify Solidity 0.8.x behavior on explicit downcasting
Create a PoC where marketSupply is reduced to 1 wei before update_market is called

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deposits into a market, then withdraws almost all liquidity, leaving 1 wei. They wait for rewards to accrue, then call update_market. The accumulator truncates and wraps. The attacker re-deposits, now earning rewards against a reset accumulator, while all previous depositors lose their pending rewards.
prerequisites: Ability to manipulate marketSupply to a very low value

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of pending rewards for all users in the affected market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors in the affected market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Impossible to recover lost rewards without governance intervention

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge case tests for extremely low marketSupply

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Requires enough capital to dominate a market and withdraw it
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Single transaction to withdraw and update
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as a sudden drop in accCantoPerShare

threats.title: Current Epoch Claiming Violates Protocol Design Intent
threats.hypothesis_description: The NatSpec for the claim function explicitly states it 'Can only be performed for prior (i.e. finished) epochs, not the current one'. However, the code lacks any check to enforce this, allowing users to claim rewards block-by-block in the current epoch.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the protocol's security model relies on delayed claiming (e.g., to prevent front-running of gauge weight changes or reward rate adjustments), this implementation flaw completely bypasses that protection.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing epoch validation check in claim function despite NatSpec

preconditions: User has accrued rewards in the current unfinished epoch

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No require statement enforcing that block.number > epoch end

analysis_questions: Does immediate claiming allow MEV extraction or front-running of governance actions?
Is this purely a documentation error or a critical missing safeguard?
validation_requirements: Review GaugeController integration to see if immediate claiming enables weight manipulation exploits

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker observes a pending transaction that will reduce their market's gauge weight. They immediately call claim() to extract rewards at the current higher weight before the epoch finishes.
prerequisites: Pending gauge weight or reward rate changes

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Unfair reward extraction by sophisticated actors

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying claim restrictions for current epochs

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Front-running specific governance or gauge transactions
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as claims immediately preceding weight changes

threats.title: Missing Scaling in secRewardsPerShare Corrupts Secondary Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: The secRewardsPerShare calculation divides by marketSupply but does not account for the underlying token's decimals, as explicitly noted by a 'TODO: Scaling' comment. For markets using non-18 decimal tokens (e.g., USDC), this heavily inflates or deflates the secondary reward accumulator.
threats.investigation_rationale: Third-party protocols relying on secRewardDebt to distribute secondary rewards will massively over-distribute or under-distribute rewards, leading to immediate drainage of their reward pools.
deployment_assumptions: Whitelisted lending markets use tokens with decimals other than 18
threats.config_evidence: Depends on the specific lending markets whitelisted by governance
preconditions: Whitelisted market uses a token with decimals != 18
Third-party protocol uses secRewardDebt for distributions

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Division by marketSupply without normalizing decimals

analysis_questions: How do integrated third-party protocols consume secRewardDebt?
Can an attacker drain a third-party protocol's reward pool using this inflation?
validation_requirements: Calculate the resulting secRewardDebt and its impact on a mock third-party distributor

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deposits into a USDC (6 decimals) lending market. The secRewardsPerShare is inflated by 10^12. The attacker claims secondary rewards from the third-party protocol, draining its entire reward pool due to the inflated debt calculation.
prerequisites: Integration with a third-party secondary reward distributor

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drainage of third-party secondary reward pools
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade and manual restitution by third-party

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests with non-18 decimal tokens

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as massive secondary reward claims

threats.title: Unbounded Gas Consumption in update_market Loop Leads to DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The update_market function contains a while loop that iterates over epochs from lastRewardBlock to block.number. Inside the loop, it makes an external state-modifying call to gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write. If a market is not updated for many epochs, the accumulated gas cost could exceed the block gas limit.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the block gas limit is exceeded, the market becomes permanently bricked. Users cannot claim rewards, and sync_ledger will revert, preventing users from withdrawing their underlying deposits from the lending market.

proof_of_signal.pattern: while loop over block numbers containing an external call

preconditions: Significant time (many epochs) has passed since lastRewardBlock

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Gas cost scales linearly with the number of missed epochs
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to process epochs in chunks or bound the loop

analysis_questions: What is the exact gas cost of gauge_relative_weight_write?
How many epochs must pass to exceed the block gas limit?
validation_requirements: Profile the gas cost of a single loop iteration
Calculate the maximum safe time gap before DoS occurs

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A low-activity market is ignored for a long period. When a user finally tries to withdraw their funds, the lending market calls sync_ledger, which calls update_market. The transaction runs out of gas, permanently locking the user's funds in the lending market.
prerequisites: Long period of inactivity for a specific market

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent freezing of user deposits in the affected lending market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors in the inactive market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires governance to upgrade the contract or manually advance lastRewardBlock

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests simulating large gaps between updates

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Precise gas profiling of the external call

threats.title: De-whitelisting a Market Permanently Freezes User Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: If governance de-whitelists a market, the update_market function will revert. Because claim() calls update_market(), users are permanently prevented from claiming rewards they already earned prior to the de-whitelisting.
threats.investigation_rationale: Administrative actions should not confiscate legitimately earned user funds. The lack of a grace period or emergency claim path means a routine governance action will unintentionally harm users.
preconditions: Users have unclaimed rewards in that market

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Strict access control blocking withdrawal paths
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No bypass for claiming past rewards when a market is deactivated

analysis_questions: Is there any alternative path for users to recover their rewards?
Does de-whitelisting also freeze withdrawals on the lending market side?
validation_requirements: Simulate a de-whitelisting event and verify that all claim attempts revert

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance decides to deprecate an old lending market and de-whitelists it. Immediately, all users who haven't claimed their CANTO rewards lose access to them. If the lending market calls sync_ledger on withdrawal, users also cannot withdraw their underlying deposits.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of earned rewards and potentially frozen underlying deposits
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users in the deprecated market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Governance must re-whitelist the market to allow claims

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for user claims after market de-whitelisting

threats.title: Re-whitelisting a Market Creates Reward Accounting Discontinuity
threats.hypothesis_description: When a market is re-whitelisted, lastRewardBlock is set to block.number, but accCantoPerShare is not reset. Users who maintained deposits during the de-whitelisted period will have their rewardDebt calculated against the old accumulator upon withdrawal, causing incorrect reward calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: State transitions for administrative actions must maintain accounting invariants. The failure to properly adjust accumulators on re-whitelisting corrupts user balances.
preconditions: Governance de-whitelists and then re-whitelists a market
Users maintained deposits during the gap

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: lastRewardBlock is updated but accCantoPerShare is left stale
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing accumulator adjustment logic for re-whitelisting

analysis_questions: How exactly does the stale accumulator affect a user's rewardDebt upon their next sync_ledger call?
Can this lead to underflows in claim()?
validation_requirements: Trace the math for a user who deposits, waits, experiences a de-whitelist/re-whitelist cycle, and then claims

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Market is de-whitelisted and later re-whitelisted. A user who deposited before the de-whitelisting calls claim(). Their rewards are calculated using the stale accumulator, potentially granting them phantom rewards or causing an underflow depending on how the market supply changed.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users who held deposits through a de-whitelist cycle
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Difficult to correct without manual state manipulation

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for the full lifecycle of de-whitelisting and re-whitelisting
